artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick update before Mam’s appointment

Hi Sean,

Just grabbing ten minutes with a coffee before I head into Park West, so I thought I’d send you a proper update instead of the usual WhatsApp voice notes.

Mam has another oncology review at the Mater Misericordiae on Thursday afternoon – they’re checking how she’s doing after that rough patch last October when I took the three weeks unpaid to bring her in every day. Between losing about €1,600 in wages and the €450 we bled on meds and parking, that’s when the November and December mortgage payments first went sideways. I kept telling myself we’d catch up the following month and, if I’m honest, I ended up paying ESB, groceries, and bits for the kids first and letting the mortgage slide.

Work is still a circus. I’m technically back in good books after that firewall misconfig on the Park West client last year, but I haven’t forgotten the written warning on 27 November and the overtime freeze from November to January. Losing that extra €450–€600 a month really knocked us. I’m also still getting the odd flutter in my chest thinking about the data breach mess – even though the internal review in June 2024 cleared me, Brian’s snide comments back in March were what kicked off the panic attacks and the GP signing me off.

Kids are grand. Aoife’s obsessed with her GAA training, and Liam’s after-school reading group is helping – he actually picked up a book himself last night, which nearly made me cry with relief.

Let me know what days you’re free to come up to Dublin next month. Maybe we can tag-team Mam’s appointments so I’m not taking all the time off.

Chat soon,
Michael

Title: Late-night money/house thoughts

Couldn’t sleep again so I’m dumping this here instead of scrolling.

The arrears have crept up over so many different things. Revenue started taking €320 a month straight from my pay in March 2024 for that 2021–2022 tax underpayment, and I just sort of shrugged and thought, “Grand, overtime will cover it.” Then the overtime at DigitalConnect got suspended after the firewall incident and the written warning on 27/11/23, and there was nothing spare to plug the gap.

On top of that we’ve had all the hospital stuff. Mam’s overnight cardiac observation at the Mater on 14 May 2024 cost €2,750 up front; I stuck it on the credit card and now I’m paying back €180 a month. Liam’s bits – Temple Street assessments for the learning stuff and asthma, plus the private consults with Dr. Niamh Kelly in Blackrock Clinic between September and December – came to about €1,200. We also had that €1,050 clutch and timing belt job on the Octavia at Murphy’s Auto in Ballyfermot when it died on the N4.

If I’m honest, I’ve been stuffing the bank’s letters into a drawer because opening them makes my chest tighten. I kept hoping a bonus or pay rise would magically appear and I’d sort it “next month”. Now it’s months later and I’m still here, staring at the ceiling worrying we’ll be told to sell up.

Need to price exactly what it would cost to move… but I really, really don’t want to uproot the kids from Castleknock and St. Brigid’s.

Subject: Emma & Liam – just to keep you in the loop

Hi Mags,

Meant to ring you at the weekend but the days disappeared in a blur of homework and hospital runs, so I’ll write it down while I’m on the train.

Emma’s holding up but she’s wrecked. Since early September she’s been on 3.5 days a week at St. Brigid’s so she can get Liam into Temple Street for the learning difficulty and asthma assessments. It’s been like that up to just before Christmas – that drop of about 25% in her pay is exactly when the mortgage started slipping. We’ve also given up waiting on the HSE list and have been going to Blackrock Clinic; between September and December the private educational psych and respiratory consults with Dr. Niamh Kelly hit around €1,200.

All of that is on top of the older stuff you know about – her anxiety after those panic attacks in the classroom and the 12 weeks Dr. Fiona Kelly at Glasnevin Family Practice signed her off for stress earlier in 2024. She keeps saying she’s afraid one more incident and Mr. Kavanagh will push her onto another PIP. I’m trying to convince her to talk to someone again, maybe through Beaumont or the Mater psychology clinic.

We’ve been leaning on Aoife Murphy in Castleknock for last-minute minding when Emma’s at Temple Street or Blackrock – easily €80–€100 extra a week. I should have cut back harder on other spending but instead I kept hoping things would settle and we’d “catch up later”.

Kids are themselves: Aoife’s flying it in 3rd class, and Liam loves his little phonics group even if he pretends he doesn’t.

Thanks again for taking them for that sleepover last month – it was the first time in ages we sat in a quiet house.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Emma & Liam – just to keep you in the loop

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft talking points for money advice call

Things to be clear about when I ring MABS / bank helpline:

– I let this drag on too long. When we first missed or underpaid a couple of months, I told myself overtime or a raise would sort it. Revenue then started that €320/month deduction in March 2024 for the old 2021–22 tax underpayment, and instead of ringing the bank straight away, I just tried to juggle everything.

– Big hits: Mam’s oncology block at the Mater in late October 2024 (three weeks unpaid leave from DigitalConnect, around €1,600 lost wages + €450 meds/parking); Mam’s overnight cardiac observation on 14/05/24 – €2,750 on the card, now €180/month repayments; car repair in November 2025 – Octavia clutch + timing belt at Murphy’s Auto in Ballyfermot for €1,050.

– Kids’ stuff: Liam’s private assessments with Dr. Niamh Kelly in Blackrock Clinic (roughly €1,200 between Sept–Dec 2025) on top of Temple Street appointments; extra childcare with Aoife Murphy when Emma was at hospital; Aoife’s bits for GAA, school tours, etc.

– Work stress hasn’t helped. Since the data breach incident at DigitalConnect that Brian tried to pin on me in March 2024, I’ve been on and off with panic attacks. GP signed me off for a spell; internal review in June 2024 cleared me, but I’ve been half-afraid of rocking the boat, which is part of why I kept ignoring early letters and calls about the arrears instead of fronting up.

Main ask: some kind of structured plan so we can clear what we owe without losing the house.

metadata.title: Draft talking points for money advice call

artifacts.content:
Subject: Work/health catch-up (long one!)

Hey mate,

Figured I’d send a proper email instead of half-baked Slack messages.

Work first: I’m still at DigitalConnect for now, but the last couple of years have taken chunks out of me. You remember the firewall misconfig on that Park West client and the written warning on 27/11/23 – they froze my overtime from November to January, so we lost about €450–€600 a month we’d been leaning on for the mortgage. Then there was the on-call rota madness: 24/7 coverage for an extra €350 a month. By late 2022 I was barely sleeping, and Dr. Fiona Kelly at Glasnevin Medical Centre started me on Zopiclone.

The worst was the data breach incident last March. Brian more or less hinted it was my fault in front of the team. I ended up signed off on stress and panic attacks; I’ve been on long-term sick since March 2024. The internal review in June 2024 finally cleared me of negligence, but the damage was done. I honestly don’t know if I can walk back into Park West as “Senior Network Engineer” like nothing happened.

Money-wise, the hit from losing overtime and then being on sick pay is part of why the mortgage is in bits. On top of that we’ve had Mam’s oncology stuff at the Mater, Liam’s Temple Street and Blackrock appointments, plus that €2,750 Mater bill for her overnight cardiac observation last May that I stuck on a card.

Part of me thinks the sensible thing would be to sell the house and downsize, but the idea of hauling the kids out of Castleknock and St. Brigid’s makes my stomach drop. I’m clinging to the hope we can sort some arrangement instead.

Anyway, enough doom and gloom. How’s life on your side? Still thinking about that move to Canada?

Cheers,
Mick

Title: Liam – hospital/clinic & Emma work schedule (Sept–Dec 2025)

Recurring notes to keep my head straight:

Every Monday (Sept–Dec 2025)
Time: 14:30–17:30
Event: Liam – Temple Street CHI assessment block
Location: Children’s Health Ireland at Temple Street, Dublin 1
Notes: Emma on reduced hours (3.5 days/week at St. Brigid’s) so she can bring him. Remember inhalers, school reports, and list of concerns about reading/phonics.

Every second Wednesday (Sept–Dec 2025)
Time: 15:00–18:00
Event: Liam – Blackrock Clinic private consults
Location: Blackrock Clinic, Co. Dublin
Notes: Educational psych + respiratory with Dr. Niamh Kelly. Approx €300 per visit; total expected ~€1,200 by December. Check credit card limit beforehand.

Ad hoc entries:

“Extra childcare – Aoife M. (Castleknock)” – usually 13:00–18:00 on days Emma is at Temple Street or Blackrock. Budget €80–€100 per week. These weeks are what pushed us over the edge on December’s mortgage.

“Car to Murphy’s Auto – clutch/timing belt” – 08:00–17:00, one day in November 2025
Location: Murphy’s Auto Services, Ballyfermot
Notes: Breakdown on N4 near Liffey Valley. Cost €1,050 on credit card; prioritised this over full November mortgage so Emma can get to school and hospital.

Also flag: Mam’s oncology review block in late October at Mater Misericordiae University Hospital – took three weeks unpaid leave from DigitalConnect in 2024 for similar schedule. Don’t repeat without planning.

Title: Budget reflection – where I went wrong

Sitting at the kitchen table with the AIB statements and it’s pretty clear how I let this get out of hand.

When Sean’s drink-driving mess blew up in August 2023, I transferred €3,200 from our little mortgage buffer to his solicitor in Galway before the 18 August court date. That wiped out the cushion we’d been using to smooth over tight months. Not long after, the Child Maintenance Service in the UK wrote about an old admin error from before I met Emma – I had to pay €1,150 in back maintenance by 10 October 2023. I raided the mortgage overpayment pot for that as well.

At the same time, overtime at DigitalConnect was all over the place after the firewall incident and written warning on 27/11/23, and then Revenue started lifting €320 a month from my wages in March 2024 for the 2021–22 underpayment. Instead of facing the numbers properly, I kept telling myself, “The mortgage is the one thing we can be a bit late on; we’ll catch it up when things calm down.”

Looking back, I was prioritising every other bill and day-to-day spend over the roof over our heads. Embarrassment didn’t help – I put off talking to anyone, even you, because I didn’t want to admit I’d made such a mess of it.

Need to build a proper emergency fund once we’re out of this hole, even if it’s €50 a month to start.

metadata.title: Budget reflection – where I went wrong

artifacts.content:
Subject: Bit of a brain dump about the house/money

Hi Mam,

Kids are in bed, Emma’s marking copies at the table, and I’m sitting here staring at the AIB app, so I thought I’d write instead of stewing.

You know most of the story: overtime at DigitalConnect got cut after the firewall incident and written warning back in November 2023, then Revenue started taking €320/month from my wages in March 2024 for that old tax underpayment. Around the same time you had the angina scare – the Mater billed €2,750 for the overnight cardiac observation on 14 May 2024, which I stuck on a credit card I’m now paying back at €180 a month.

On top of that, Sean’s crash near Galway University Hospital on 12 June 2024 meant I took unpaid days in June and July and sent him about €2,750 from our joint account for rent and rehab. Between that, Liam’s private assessments with Dr. Kelly in Blackrock, and Emma cutting to 3.5 days a week at St. Brigid’s to bring him to Temple Street, the mortgage has just slipped further and further.

I’m not proud of it, but for a while I just shoved the bank’s letters in a drawer because every time I opened one my heart would race. I kept thinking, “Next month I’ll sort it,” and of course next month never came. I also dragged my heels talking to you and Dad because I was mortified I’d let it get this bad.

We’re still fighting to keep the house. I know selling and downsizing might be the sensible thing on paper, but the thought of moving the kids out of Castleknock and away from St. Brigid’s makes me feel sick.

Anyway, that’s where my head is. We’ll still be over on Sunday for dinner if that’s okay.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Bit of a brain dump about the house/money

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Andrew O’Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Byrne <sarah.byrne@example.com>
Date: 2025-02-03
Subject: Background for the mortgage arrears review

Hi Sarah,

You asked for a clear written summary ahead of the lender’s review, so I’m putting everything in one place.

I’m currently unemployed. Up to January 2024 I was working as a Senior Network Engineer with DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12. I’d already reduced to a three‑day week between September 2023 and February 2024 so I could bring Mam, Bríd O’Connor, to her chemotherapy at the Mater Hospital in Dublin 7. My manager, Brian Kavanagh, had tentatively offered me a promotion to Network Operations Lead with about an extra €8,000 a year, but I turned it down because of her treatment schedule.

On top of that, I quietly started doing some cash‑in‑hand weekend network installs for a pub in Blanchardstown in mid‑2023 to cover Liam Patrick O’Connor’s private speech therapy at a clinic in Dublin after we’d been stuck over 18 months on the HSE waitlist. Brian actually emailed me in November 2023 warning that this could breach the conflict‑of‑interest clause in my DigitalConnect contract.

Things really unravelled when HR opened a formal investigation into my remote access logs after a junior engineer on my team, Mark Doyle, misused an admin account I’d left logged in during an overnight maintenance window. That led to a mutual termination agreement in January 2024, so my employment with DigitalConnect ended at that point.

I know none of this looks great on paper, but I want the lender to see the full context.

Best,
Michael
Willowbank Drive, Castleknock, Dublin 15

metadata.subject: Background for the mortgage arrears review

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Andrew O’Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: “Mags” Margaret Byrne <margaret.byrne@example.com>
Date: 2025-02-10
Subject: What’s been going on with Emma and the mortgage

Hi Mags,

I know you’ve been worried and only getting bits and pieces from Emma, so I’ll spell it out.

Emma is officially unemployed now – her resignation from St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, took effect on 15 January 2025. She’d been a 3rd class primary teacher there, working 27.5 hours per week up to June 2024, when she accepted a voluntary redundancy package of about €18,000 after an April 2024 meeting where they warned her post might be suppressed next year because of projected enrolment drops and Department of Education budget cuts.

That came after a really rough spell. Between January and April 2024 she was on extended certified sick leave from St. Brigid’s with severe postnatal depression, diagnosed by Dr. Keane at the HSE mental health clinic in Blanchardstown. She was only on half‑rate sick pay, so our income dropped by around €900 a month and we fell behind on the February and March mortgage instalments. Then between March and June 2024 she stayed on reduced hours so she could attend weekly CBT sessions every Thursday afternoon at Beaumont Hospital’s mental health unit – €95 per session plus extra childcare.

Before that, from September 2022 to May 2024, she’d already been on a 0.6 full‑time equivalent timetable so she could use Wednesday afternoons and Fridays for counselling at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital psychology clinic, after that awful incident where a parent assaulted her outside the school gate.

It’s been one thing after another, but we’re trying to get back on an even keel.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: What’s been going on with Emma and the mortgage

artifacts.content:
Google Keep note – drafted by Michael O’Connor
Last edited: 2025-02-15
Title: Emma – employment history + issues (for forms / advisors)

Emma Louise O’Connor – currently unemployed.

Previous role: 3rd class primary school teacher, St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock.

Key points to remember (DO NOT share everything unless specifically asked):

• Sept 2022 – May 2024: Worked on a reduced 0.6 full‑time equivalent timetable. Used Wednesday afternoons and Fridays to attend ongoing counselling sessions at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital psychology clinic in Dublin, following assault by a parent outside the school gate.

• Sept 2023: Board of Management at St. Brigid’s opened an internal safeguarding investigation after a pupil with additional needs briefly went missing from her classroom during yard transition. Emma took a career break starting September 2023 while the safeguarding complaint was looked into. Career break approval letter mentions “internal safeguarding investigation” – keep copy for union / INTO.

• Sept 2024: Placed on a formal performance improvement plan (PIP) by principal Mr. Kavanagh, mainly linked to stress, absences and paperwork delays when Aoife Grace O’Connor was being assessed for ADHD at Temple Street Children’s Hospital, Dublin.

• 15 Jan 2025: Effective date Emma became officially unemployed – she resigned from St. Brigid’s following the PIP and ongoing stress.

When explaining on forms: stick to neutral language – “career break for family reasons”, “resigned for health and family care reasons”, avoid explicit mention of assault, safeguarding investigation, ADHD assessment unless absolutely required.

metadata.title: Emma – employment history + issues (for forms / advisors)
